Child Exploitation Material (CEM)

Investigation and Prosecution Guide

Version 1.3

Amended Detective Inspector McKenzie 1.3 4/01/2022

APPENDIX 1 – SAMPLE ANNEXURE AND CAVEAT TO STATEMENT….Page 10 APPENDIX 2 – SAMPLE INVESTIGATOR STATEMENT….………………. Page 11 Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3

OVERVIEW

This document serves as a guide to the Western Australia Police Force (WA Police) Prosecuting Division, the Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions (ODPP) and the Commonwealth Director for Public Prosecutions (CDPP) in regards to the way WA Police will prepare evidentiary material for court use where Child Exploitation Material (CEM) is located and analysed.

This has been implemented following the decision by WA Police to align with other Australian Law Enforcement Agencies by adopting the Australian Child Abuse Categorisation Schema or ACACS in place of the current 9-point ANVIL (otherwise known as Oliver) Scale. The ACACS is modelled on the INTERPOL Baseline for classifying CEM.

This decision has been made after consideration of a report prepared by the Queensland Sentencing Advisory Council (QSAC)1 and acknowledges the clear occupational health and safety risk to investigators and members of the criminal justice system posed by the onerous current requirements to view, classify and then provide detailed descriptions of CEM for prosecution purposes.

The change to the ACACS will allow a transition from the current time consuming manual CEM examination processes to using purpose designed computer programs to automatically analyse seized images which are then compared with known international CEM databases.

In addition, WA Police have introduced limits to the quantity of images and video files that will be classified (per device) in order to limit the exposure of staff (wherever possible) to harmful CEM examinations.

The new standardised CEM evidence package will provide information as to the nature of the suspect’s involvement in the alleged offending and the potential risk they pose to the public. It will also provide a condensed examination report regarding the nature of the seized CEM however, it will not break down the seized images into distinct detailed categories, as has previously been the case. This evidence package will include:

• Court Brief

• Field Triage Reports

• Arresting Officers Statement – including a CEM Analysis Report and Annexure

• Classification of CEM report (ACACS – 4 category scale)

Page 11 Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3 OVERVIEW This document serves as a guide to the Western Australia Police Force (WA Police) Prosecuting Division, the Office of the Director of Public Prosecutions (ODPP) and the Commonwealth Director for Public Prosecutions (CDPP) in regards to the way WA Police will prepare evidentiary material for court use where Child Exploitation Material (CEM) is located and analysed. This has been implemented following the decision by WA Police to align with other Australian Law Enforcement Agencies by adopting the Australian Child Abuse Categorisation Schema or ACACS in place of the current 9-point ANVIL (otherwise known as Oliver) Scale. The ACACS is modelled on the INTERPOL Baseline for classifying CEM. This decision has been made after consideration of a report prepared by the Queensland Sentencing Advisory Council (QSAC)1 and acknowledges the clear occupational health and safety risk to investigators and members of the criminal justice system posed by the onerous current requirements to view, classify and then provide detailed descriptions of CEM for prosecution purposes. The change to the ACACS will allow a transition from the current time consuming manual CEM examination processes to using purpose designed computer programs to automatically analyse seized images which are then compared with known international CEM databases. In addition, WA Police have introduced limits to the quantity of images and video files that will be classified (per device) in order to limit the exposure of staff (wherever possible) to harmful CEM examinations. The new standardised CEM evidence package will provide information as to the nature of the suspect’s involvement in the alleged offending and the potential risk they pose to the public. It will also provide a condensed examination report regarding the nature of the seized CEM however, it will not break down the seized images into distinct detailed categories, as has previously been the case. This evidence package will include: • Court Brief • Field Triage Reports • Arresting Officers Statement – including a CEM Analysis Report and Annexure • Classification of CEM report (ACACS – 4 category scale)

sections.h:
The Court Brief will summarise the investigation and include:

• How the suspect came to the attention of police

• How police identified the suspect

• The nature of the relationship between the suspect and the victim (if known)

1 Classification of child exploitation material for sentencing purposes: Final report Published by Queensland Sentencing Advisory Council, July 2017.

Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3

• The nature of the suspect’s involvement in the matter (including overt acts)

• Any admissions made by the suspect

• Any other charges laid (such as contact sexual offending)

• Details of any co-accused (where possible)

• The nature of any ongoing investigations (where possible to disclose)

• The conduct of any search warrants

• Details of any relevant internet searches made by the suspect

• Details of any relevant file paths, file types or file names linked to seized CEM

• The level of compliance of the suspect in relation to the provision of passwords or

compliance with Data Access Orders.

• Results of any field triage of CEM

• General investigator observations (where relevant) regarding any CEM located

• Any use of anonymising or encryption software (designed to prevent detection)

• Perceived risk to the community (or themselves)

• Detailed bail submissions

• Any other relevant information or supporting documents for the prosecutor

This is intended to provide prosecutors with sufficient accurate information to properly progress matters through the court system, with a particular emphasis on describing the nature and context of the alleged offending, any risk to the community and relevant bail submissions.

sections.h:
Wherever possible, an automated triage process will be undertaken in the field to examine electronic devices and any interaction with these devices by the suspect. The Field Triage Reports will provide supporting evidence when an investigator has observed that CEM exists on a seized device. The types of reports will be as follows:

• Advanced Digital Forensics (ADF Triage)

This system is primarily used for the automated field examination of electronic storage devices to identify CEM on devices that are powered ‘on’ or capable of accepting power (such as USB’s or external hard drives). There is also some capacity to remove and examine devices that are not powered, such as internal hard drives, although this cannot be undertaken in the field. Further in-depth examination of any information extracted can be undertaken in an examination suite, however this may cause some delays for prosecutors (especially around first court appearances) as this information may not yet be available.

At the completion of the analysis, the investigator can provide an electronic .html report that is provided on USB, disc or by email. These reports are provided as a separate document for each device. Typically, the ADF Triage system will be used in the field to simply identify the presence of CEM on devices, which are then seized for further analysis. This reduces the unnecessary seizure of devices where no CEM is detected.

This also means that the Field Triage Report is typically completed sometime after the initial examination, as opposed to during the initial search in the field.

Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3

• Universal Forensic Extraction Device (UFED) otherwise known as Cellebrite

This is typically used for mobile phones or tablet/hand held devices and can also be used for some portable storage devices. At the completion of the analysis, the investigator can provide an electronic .html report that is provided on USB, disc or by email. These reports are provided as a separate document for each device. Utilising these forensic tools, investigators can ‘tag’ and comment on any relevant material to substantiate charges (i.e. the possession of CEM) and can be used to quickly identify CEM and suspicious items located in the field. It also enables located devices to be excluded from further time-consuming examinations when no CEM is located. Each report can provide the investigator with the opportunity to summarised electronic .html report of any offending located in the field.

Field Triage Reports can be provided to prosecutors at a much earlier stage of the investigation to substantiate a prima-facie case. Exactly when a Field Triage Report can be provided depends on:

• Whether the software was successful in the field

• Whether additional (more detailed) examination is necessary (or possible)

• Whether a specialist examination or expert assistance is required (for whatever

reason)

• The number and type of devices seized

Field Triage Reports will contain CEM, so they can only be made available for viewing by defence counsel (and not served as part of disclosure).

sections.h: This document is provided to demonstrate the presumptive presence of CEM on each exhibit identified during the field triage process. It is not intended as a forensic evidentiary report, but an evidentiary guide to help understand the evidence identified during the investigation. This will be provided with the Investigating Officer’s (IO’s) statement and will form part of the brief of evidence.
sections.b: This document is provided to demonstrate the presumptive presence of CEM on each exhibit identified during the field triage process. It is not intended as a forensic evidentiary report, but an evidentiary guide to help understand the evidence identified during the investigation. This will be provided with the Investigating Officer’s (IO’s) statement and will form part of the brief of evidence.

sections.h:
The primary investigating officer’s statement will include information concerning the investigation and how the defendant came to police attention (as per the Court Brief section above). It will address the investigative actions of the police including references to the in- field triage of devices and references to any CEM. It will address the level of co-operation by the accused through the provision (or not) of passwords and compliance with any Data Access Orders.

To assist with any sentencing considerations, the statement will include information to address the:

• Nature of the Offending – including a general summary of the evidence, any

relationship between the suspect and the victim, the circumstances surrounding the possession, distribution, accessing or producing of CEM as well as the alleged source of the CEM and whether it has been transmitted or shared with others.

Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3

• Nature of the CEM – providing an overall description of the nature and content of

the material including (but not limited to) the estimated number of unique victims and their apparent age, the general type (or types) of activity depicted and the extent of cruelty or physical harm. A detailed description of a small number of representative images or videos is required, but it is not necessary to provide a fully detailed or itemised description of all seized CEM.

The Investigator Statement must follow the template attached as Appendix 2.

CLASSIFICATION of CEM REPORT

The “Griffeye” software also used by WA Police is a categorisation tool used by law enforcement agencies around the world and offers a purpose-built forensic tool that allows categorisation under the new (4 tier) ACACS. The ACACS has been adopted across Australia to ensure consistency, to allow integration with international law enforcement CEM databases and to better assist with victim identification. Two of the four categorises are illegal in WA.

The ACACS has been adopted across Australia to ensure consistency, to allow integration with international law enforcement CEM databases and to better assist with victim identification. Two of the four categorises are illegal in WA.

sections.h:
Category Child Exploitation Material Guide

(CEM) Category Description

1 CEM – Baseline • Must depict a pre-pubescent child (or one very early into puberty) who is perceived to be under 13 years of age.

• Must be a “real child”.

• Must show sexual activity involving a child (including having a

child watch sexual activity) or;

la • Have a clear focus on either the genitals or anus.

g

e

llI

2 CEM – Any Other CEM Any other CEM that meets the Western Australian or Australian Commonwealth legislative definitions of CEM that is not category 1, including;

• Animated or written CEM

• Children over the age of 13 years, including

Commonwealth Child Abuse Material (CAM) with children

under the age of 18 years.

3 Related Investigative Non-illegal related child material which is;

Interest – Not Illegal but • Believed to form part of a series containing CEM Connected • Of investigative interest

la

g

e

L 4 Ignorable Anything else, including;

• Adult pornography

• Windows artefacts

• Files unable to be determined

At the completion of the analysis, the investigator can provide an electronic .html report that is provided on USB, disc or by email. One significant advantage is that Griffeye reports can be provided as one combined document for all seized devices. A user guide (such as above) will be included in the brief of evidence to assist with the interpretation of any CEM examination reports.

Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3

PROCESS

Seized devices subject to a Field Triage Report can still be subject to further specialist examination by qualified technical experts in a manner and location endorsed by WA Police Technology Crime Services (TCS). Typically, devices will be uploaded using Griffeye software, which will allow investigators to commence a more in-depth analysis to identify additional evidence including; locating CEM files possessed and accessed, timeframes of offending, frequency and method of CEM distribution and frequency of access to material, as well as the task of classifying the material seized. Whether any seized device examinations are limited to Field Triage Reports or further in-depth expert analysis will be determined on a case by case basis.

When evidence has been classified by the investigator against the (4 tier) ACACS, a categorisation report is requested by the investigator from TCS. Once that report is made available, the investigator can provide the report which sets out the total number of images or videos in each category that have been categorised. This report can then be provided to the prosecuting authority. Importantly, the TCS Report is not a witness statement or evidentiary certificate, but is an aid to the court (particularly for sentencing) to assist with providing an understanding of the CEM possessed or created by the accused person.

sections.h:
Unless there is:

• Clear concerns for contact offending

• A reasonable possibility of victim identification

• An opportunity to use automated CEM categorisation software

WA Police have adopted a policy of only manually categorising 1000 CEM images or 50 CEM video files per device. The per device policy will ensure there is sufficient grounds to obtain an order for destruction for each of the seized devices where CEM is located. Where this policy is followed, police will only allege the quantity of CEM that is categorised, as under current legislative provisions, the court cannot draw an inference regarding how many additional CEM images may exist within material that has not been classified. This policy has been implemented to ensure WA Police comply with their Occupational Safety and Health obligations by limiting harmful CEM exposure to staff both internally and across the broader criminal justice system. As detailed earlier in this document, the brief of evidence should provide a detailed account of all relevant factors in the case to assist the court for sentencing purposes.

TIMEFRAMES

Examinations of devices will be prioritised based on levels of perceived community risk.

Timeframes to complete examinations may also vary considerably, and often depend on a number of factors which include the volume of information to be reviewed, the number of distinct devices seized, any technical difficulties which may be associated with encryption or password protections and the availability of suitably qualified staff to attend to each of the specific tasks. Should Commonwealth “accessing” charges be preferred, this will also require a longer examination time due to extra processes that are required for these types of offences. All inquiries as to timeframes should be directed to the Investigating Officer in Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3 the first instance, or otherwise through his or her supervisor. Inquiries are not to be made directly to TCS.

Field Triage Reports may, or may not be available for inclusion on the prosecution brief at first appearance. This will depend on a variety of factors, including the complexity of the matter. This report (once available) may however be disclosed by the investigating officer at any time, or when requested. More detailed examination reports are available for disclosure once received, however timelines for these reports are subject to many variables, making a defined time period impossible to comply with. In regards to full trial reports being provided by TCS, unless there are significant extenuating circumstances associated with a particular matter, these reports will only be prepared when a trial date is set. These reports will be provided in sufficient time to ensure WA Police comply with disclosure obligations under the Criminal Procedure Act.

CONTACT

All queries in relation to the adoption of these guidelines should be directed to the Detective Senior Supervisor – Taskforce Odessa, through the following email address:

ChildAbuseSquadSMAIL@police.wa.gov.au

Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3

MEMORANDUM

sections.t: Subject: Annexure and Caveat to Statement
sections.h:
CAVEAT

This document is provided to demonstrate the presumptive presence of Child Exploitation Material (CEM) on the subject equipment during field testing. It is not intended as a forensic report, and therefore should not solely rely on its content to prove or disprove the presence of CEM. A detailed forensic analysis expert report prepared by WA Police Technology Crime Services (TCS) is required to provide scientific evidence.

This document and its contents remain the property of the WA Police Force. It is not to be further disseminated to any third party without proper authorisation. It is to be used for law enforcement and judicial processes only. Possession of this document and / or inappropriate disclosure of its contents may be an offence if it is not directly related to these official functions.

sections.b: Date: Contact Number: Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3

sections.h:
First Name, Surname

STATES

1. This statement is designed as a guide only and not as an

exhaustive list of all possible inclusions. Officers should seek advice where necessary and ensure that all reasonable

information relevant to each specific incident is presented to the court to assist with the judicial process.

2. This statement should be accompanied by an Annexure and

Caveat to Statement form and should also be completed in accordance with the WA Police Child Exploitation Material

(CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide.

3. I am a RANK currently stationed at LOCATION.

4. Identify On DATE I received information from SOURCE in relation to

HOW the suspect (or the accused) person, who I now know to be SUSPECT NAME came to police attention. Please

articulate the nature of the referral (i.e. international referral, Crime Stoppers report, online engagement etc) which

indicated the suspect had been utilising TYPE of platform/s being used to download / transmit / access CEM.

5. DETAIL any inquiries made to test this information.

6. A Criminal Investigation Act 2006 (CIA) Search Warrant was

subsequently obtained for ADDRESS.

7. Exhibit I now produce this Search Warrant.

8. On DATE I was rostered to perform DUTIES in company with

CORROBORATING OFFICER/s etc.

Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3

9. At TIME, I attended at ADDRESS, with OFFICER DETAILS.

DETAIL the execution of the Search Warrant.

10. At the commencement of this Search Warrant, NAME

activated a video recording device. (also consider BWC

footage).

11. Exhibit I now produce this recording.

12. The OCCUPANT/S were provided with a copy of the Search

Warrant (or detail the alternative power used such as

Informed Consent under section 30, a Senior Officer Order under section 133 or other legislative powers) and

compliance with any relevant CIA REQUIREMENTS (i.e. the requirement for policer to identify themselves under section 11 and Occupier’s Rights as per section 31 etc).

13. DETAIL whether they were arrested, and if so, what power

was used, whether they were searched including what power was used, and any Police Cautions or CIA Rights (i.e.

Accompanying Persons under section 28 or Arrested

Suspects as per sections 137, 138 etc) that were provided or afforded).

14. During the search of the premises DESCRIBE EXHIBIT was

located. This was recorded as EXHIBIT NUMBER. DETAIL

of photographs (if taken).

15. Exhibit I now produce this EXHIBIT

16. With the assistance of TRIAGE OFFICER field triaging of

number / type devices was carried out utilising the triage tool ADF Triage (etc).

17. During the examination of DEVICE / EXHIBIT NUMBER a

preliminary ADF Triage (etc) report was produced. (Briefly describe any evidence identified during the triage of the exhibit. Copy for each relevant exhibit).

18. Exhibit I now produce the Preliminary Triage Report.

Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3

19. During the field triage process, police located encryption /

password protection on NAME DEVICES / ACCOUNTS. A

request was made to provide access information including usernames and passwords. These details were PROVIDED

or DECLINED by the suspect.

20. A Data Access Order (if applicable) was served on the

suspect who PROVIDED or DECLINED to provide the

requested particulars.

21. The suspect DID or DID NOT participate in a field interview

where he / she ARTICULATE KEY POINTS (i.e. made

admissions etc).

22. Exhibit I now produce this recording.

23. Please also consider Body Worn Camera (BWC) Footage.

24. Exhibit I now produce these recordings.

25. As a result of the field triaging, NUMBER of DEVICES were

seized, including specific devices related to offences. Exhibit Log NUMBER refers which WAS / WAS NOT endorsed by

the suspect (or IMS report if recorded directly).

26. Exhibit I now produce this Exhibits Log.

27. Exhibit I now produce the relevant devices (if required, as per the

finalised UPML).

28. I later completed an Annexure and Caveat to Statement in

relation to this matter.

29. Exhibit I now produce this document.

30. Upon completion of the search, the suspect DID or DID NOT

participate in a formal EROI where he made admissions,

provided no comment etc.

31. Exhibit I now produce this EROI

Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3

32. The suspect (or accused) was charged with the listed

offences and BAILED, CONVEYED TO PWH etc.

33. The seized exhibits were lodged and a Request for Analysis

by Technology Crime Services (TCS) was completed. (or

further examination was undertaken using ADF Triage etc, as may happen at JACET).

34. I have had no further contact with the suspect / accused (or

otherwise detail any further contact).

35. The below sections “Nature of Offending” and “Nature of

Images” are designed to assist prosecuting authorities at sentencing. All responses must contain only facts which can be proven by evidence. No opinion evidence is to be included, unless it is admissible as “expert” evidence.

36. Nature of Offending

37. What was possessed?

38. What was found on each device? What (if any) triage tools

were used? (e.g. evidence of access of CEM from a paid web site which was possessed on an encrypted drive in multiple labelled folders etc).

39. If CEM was located, how was it possessed? (e.g. encrypted

hard drive, mobile phone, cloud storage etc). Also highlight how it was accessible.

40. How were the relevant devices linked to the suspect? (it is

important to establish that the suspect used the device and to exclude any other potential persons unless they are also to be charged). Ways to do this include detailing the use of passwords, where the device was located, who had access to the devices, any admissions made, the presence of

identifying personal files within devices such as photographs, internet banking or emails and details of IP address or internet account holdings etc).

Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3

41. Does the evidence suggest a particular sexual interest? (e.g.

the use of specific internet search terms, file names accessed with an emphasis on the predominant age and gender of the victims).

42. The time period over which the material was collected.

43. How well organised is the collection? (e.g. how many folders,

how sorted etc).

44. How was the material obtained? (such as peer to peer sites,

encrypted messaging apps, internet downloads, sent from another user etc).

45. Did the material indicate any direct grooming of victims or

solicitation from the child victims involved?

46. Did the material contain any acts which would be defined as

particularly degrading, obscene or cruel or involve animals or other devices? (in a manner that could take the offence outside of the normal sentencing range).

47. Were other items of concern located? (such as bestiality or

other obscene publications such as torture films etc).

48. Was the CEM directly obtained from children or from another

third party? (Did the suspect produce, film or create the CEM themselves?).

49. Has the suspect also been charged with other contact sexual

offences as part of the same incident or investigation? (this can be merged into a combined statement if charged on the same brief).

50. Did the suspect procure or obtain the CEM directly from the

original (contact) offender of from another source?

51. Has the suspect engaged with other suspected offenders

online including whether the suspect encouraged the

production? (e.g. active involvement in a network or process Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3 that procures, incites or encourages the creation or sharing of CEM).

52. Payment or other benefit (including an exchange of material)

was made, provided or received for the acquisition of the images.

53. Any risk of the material being seen (or otherwise shown to)

vulnerable people or children?

54. Did the suspect distribute any CEM? If so, how? (also

consider the transmit / distribute points for Commonwealth Offences as per below).

55. Did the suspect attempt to dispose of or conceal the

evidence?

56. Are there any other relevant factors for sentencing? (such as

being a person in authority or a lineal relative etc).

57. Are there any children at the suspect’s home or workplace (or

other place) where the suspect has regular access to

children?

58. On DATE I caused a Child Assessment Interview (CAI) to be

conducted (if applicable) by INTERVIEWER NAME/S. Briefly summarise the outcome (i.e. the child made disclosures or no disclosures etc).

59. On DATE I caused a Specialist Investigative Interview (SII) to

be conducted (if applicable) by INTERVIEWER NAME.

Briefly summarise the outcome (i.e. the child made

disclosures or no disclosures etc). These Interviews will generally be presented by the interviewing officers).

60. Briefly also highlight any other child protection concerns.

61. Has a hidden network or anonymising service been used in

committing the offence? Did the storage of the material involve a form of encryption?

Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3

62. If files were deleted, what was the dates of deletion?

63. Were any deleted files accessible or recoverable?

64. Was the suspect cooperative? (e.g. providing passwords

etc).

65. Did the suspect display any remorse or relevant emotions?

66. Commonwealth Offences (only)

67. Is there evidence of access? (if not, is there evidence of

attempts to conceal access such as the installation of hard drive scrubbers?). If so, please also indicate when they were installed and whether the hard drive contains evidence that the software has been effective, e.g. by no history being available.

68. Is there any other circumstantial evidence consistent with

accessing Commonwealth defined Child Abuse Material

(CAM) such as the use of CAM related internet search terms,

web browser history, the existence of Torrent files or other link files that don’t show CAM but have names that are

indicative of CAM or the access of albums known to contain

CAM?

69. What search terms have been used and what albums have

been accessed?

70. What material has been accessed? (and what format, i.e. still

images, video files etc).

71. Over what period and with what frequency has the CAM been

accessed? Hs there been an identifiable escalation in access over time? Or an escalation in the depravity or level obscenity of the CAM accessed over time?

72. Is there any evidence that the suspect paid to access the

material? If so, how?

Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3

73. Has the suspect made any admissions to accessing the

CAM? If so, what material, from where, over what length of time, and with what frequency etc?

74. Has the suspect caused CAM to be transmitted / sent to

himself?

75. How was it transmitted and/or solicited?

76. What was transmitted? Include details of the material (using

the relevant Interpol Baseline category) and the estimated age and gender of the victims.

77. On how many occasions and over what period, was the

material transmitted to the suspect or solicited by the suspect? (if on more than 3 occasions, with 2 or more

individuals each time, provide details to address the

aggravated offence under the Commonwealth Criminal Code

– s.474.24A. Officers should consult with the CDPP prior to

commencement of this charge and are encouraged to seek

advice regarding any other Commonwealth offences).

Procedural (not legal) advice can also be sought from AFP agents attached to the WAPOL JACET.

78. Who has sent it? Or from whom has it been solicited?

79. Has the suspect engaged directly with an under 18-year-old

and caused them / solicited them to send CAM of

themselves? If so;

80. How old is the child?

81. Does the evidence suggest the child is located overseas? (if

so, consider if s. 272 or 272 of the Commonwealth Criminal Code may apply).

82. Is it someone the suspect knows? Is the suspect in physical

proximity to them? Is there a sexual relationship or attempts to develop a sexual relationship? (if that person is under Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3 16yrs also consider and address groom, procure or indecent communication offences if applicable).

83. Does it appear that any threats, psychological pressure or

payment have been made, including payment via alternative currencies, virtual gifts or other means?

84. Has the suspect engaged directly with someone who has

access to an under 18-year-old and caused them / solicited them to send pornographic material of that child?

85. Does the evidence suggest the child is located overseas? (if

so, consider if s. 272 or 272 of the Commonwealth Criminal Code may apply).

86. Has payment been made?

87. Does the evidence suggest the material has been made

specifically for the suspect?

88. Has anything been given in exchange? (which can include

the exchange of CAM material, virtual gifts or alternative currencies).

89. Any evidence of the suspect transmitting CAM to others?

90. Any evidence the CAM is of someone the sender is proximate

to or that the suspect has encouraged its production?

91. Has the suspect transmitted CAM to others?

92. How was it transmitted, distributed or made available? (i.e.

which platform or medium was used?). Was a hidden network or anonymising service used to transmit the CAM?

93. What was transmitted, distributed or made available?

(photos, videos, print, category and quantity).

94. Who was it transmitted, distributed or made available to?

95. How does the suspect know the recipient (or does he know

the recipient?).

Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3

96. If made available, was it made publicly available, or available

to a selected group?

97. Were any of the recipients, or people to whom the material

was made available children?

98. Was there any benefit to the suspect in transmitting,

distributing or making available the CAM? (have they

received anything in return?).

99. Has the suspect made any admissions in relation to

transmitting / distributing / making available CAM? If so, to how many people, over what time period and on how many

occasions?

100. If the suspect is alleged to have made CAM available using a

peer to peer network, have they made any admissions to

being aware of how a peer to peer network works? i.e. that a download of a file simultaneously results in an upload of a file? Otherwise, is there any other evidence the investigator is able to give, through which such understanding could be inferred (for example if the P2P site “Transmission” is used, the fact that the download screen also shows that the user that the file is uploading).

101. Nature of Images and Videos

102. To assist with sentencing, the investigator must provide a

general description of the nature of the material contained in the collection. This can only relate to items that have been viewed. The Court cannot take notice of opinion or

assumptions made around un-viewed material, or consider the quantity of images and videos which have not been

viewed. (i.e. if we are alleging 1000 images and 50 video files from a device where another 50,000 images are yet to be viewed, the Court cannot draw any inference as to what the remaining images may (or may not) contain. It can only base its decisions on the alleged material and the surrounding information provided by the investigator.

Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3

103. Information provided by the investigator can be what is

contained within the relevant examination report, and should also address the estimated number of children depicted, their general age and gender, and a brief description of the activity involved. It should also include whether the children depicted are human children or animations (category 1 or 2), the extent of any cruelty, physical harm or other acts of a particularly obscene or degrading nature. Any other aggravating factors should also be included. Please refer to the WA Police

Investigation and Prosecution Guide for a suggested list of relevant factors.

104. A suggested wording for a CEM charge could be “During the

investigation, I have observed that the accused’s CEM library was a mixture of still images and video files. The CEM library contained still images of erotically posed children ranging in age from 5 years to 15 years of age where they were clearly exposing their breasts, vaginas and anuses. In one

photograph, a young female child, approximately 3 – 4 years old, is naked with her right leg in the air, exposing her vagina.

In other photographs, young children are depicted with

ejaculate on their lips and face. The CEM library also

contained movies showing the vaginal, anal and oral

penetration of both female and male children ranging in age from 3 years to 16 years of age by both adult male and adult female offenders. These files were clearly and accurately labelled with the ages and descriptions of offences being committed in their titles. A sample of these videos that I observed contained the forced sexual penetration of female and male toddlers (aged 4-7 years) and the humiliation of those children with titles such as “explicit name” etc. In one video of 36 seconds, a young child in a bath, estimated to be about 4 years of age, is performing fellatio on an adult male.

The child is clearly distressed and crying.

105. Exhibit I now produce this sample.

Child Exploitation Material (CEM) Investigation and Prosecution Guide V1.3

106. I declare that this statement is true to the best of my

knowledge and belief and I that I have made this statement knowing that if it is tendered in evidence I will be guilty of a crime if I have wilfully included in this statement anything which I know to be false or that I do not believe to be true.

Signed

OFFICER

sections.h:
AUTHORITY TO RELEASE INFORMATION/SPECIMENS TO THE POLICE

I, ____________________________________________ (name) _________________ (date of birth)

of,______________________________________________________________________(address)

sections.h:
Sexual abuse occurs when a child is exposed to, or involved in, sexual activity that is inappropriate to the child’s age and developmental level. It also includes situations where the child is exploited or where the child has been bribed, threatened, or coerced. Such as:-

• letting a child watch or read pornography;

• allowing a child to watch sexual acts;

• fondling the child’s genitals;

• having oral sex with a child;

• vaginal or anal penetration;

• using the internet to find a child for sexual exploitation.

Possible signs of sexual abuse include when a child:

• acts in a sexualised way that is inappropriate to his/her age;

• has pain, bleeding or swelling in his/her genital area;

• starts doing things they have grown out of such as crying a lot, bed wetting

or soiling, clinging to caregiver;

• has nightmares or sudden unexplained fears ;

• has a sexually transmitted infection or is pregnant.

If you are a victim of child abuse or paedophilia, or if you have information about someone else being abused, contact police on 131 444.

You can also report child abuse to the Child Abuse Squad on 9428 1500 or email us, to Crime Stoppers on 1800 333 000 or to your local police station.

www.police.wa.gov.au
